author: @Z3n1tHL0rD
text: I made a rocket stove , copying Roberts design with the 4 inch pipe and cast pipe fittings, then had the exhaust going through an old Gas bottle with 5 90 degree bends incorporated into the pipes inside the gas cannister, so it goes in turns 90 goes up to the top added 2 more 90's then it goes down the other side added 2 more 90's at the bottom so it goes out the other side then points upwards and into the exhaust,  I lined the cast pipes with stainless steel exhaust pipes and the 90's were all from an exhaust pipe supplier, inside the gas bottle and around the pipe inside there I put a bag or 2 of sand,the stainless pipes get up to 800 degrees Celsius, and after it warms up there is no smoke, a bucket full of wood lasts for about 4 hours, and the sand battery part stays hot for hours longer, I will probably put a copper pipe coil around the external exhaust in the future and use it to heat water, its going in my van conversion eventually, RIP Robert, A great teacher and a lovely bloke

author: @joeatnara7518
text:
A stirling engine can also provide mechanical energy, think big flywheel turning the pump that sends the cold water thru the sand battery to get hot. In fact if you can get the cold water to the cold driving piston your stirling engine will be more efficient since it is powered by the difference in temp hot side to cold side. Next you get yourself some solar panels or wind generators to pump heat back into the sand. 
If the sand battery loses too much heat the temp difference goes down proving less torque to the flywheel which pumps the cold water more slowly.

author: @GigMaresh
text:
6:50 rob the heating elements out of ovens relegated to the spring clean up pile. Then wire a car battery to them and place them in a 2 gallon metal water pail filled with sand and recharge the car battery in the day time with a solar panel. That control knob for the oven makes a great temperature control for the system. Fir heat dispersion, a 4" UBS fan into the 12 volt with a cigarette lighter socket and the UBS plug. An bag of playground sand at the lumber yard here in the U.S. is about $4
I'm so cheap. And yes, I cruise rubbish piles in the spring 😁
However, I have made a sand battery in a 14" SPARK tiny pot bellied stove and also in my 32" BUCK WARNER pot belly stove. My next feat will be in a decorative 17" milk can.
I love my ambience . . .

author: @rogerdurand9362
text: This discussion, video, and idea is very interesting.  I wish there was a forum to discuss this topic further.  We are approaching the fall in New England.  I live in a 750 SF condo unit, in a well-constructed renovated brick building and was thinking of providing heat through a sand battery.  This is my idea.  I will use a "counter oven" for the purpose (22 quarts), right on my granite counter...filled with sand and intertwined copper fittings strung together with a copper cable presenting two levels in the sand.  My idea is to heat the sand to a temp that will strongly turn a fire place fan and then calculate the rate of heat dissipation leaving the counter oven, while leaving the oven on, but controlled by a timer so that the heating element replenishes heat in the sand in a cycle.  This will take some trial and observation.  Should this work well?  My ordinary means of heat is an electric heat pump (2-year-old condensor).  Any feedback would be appreciated!  I am actively trying to learn.

author: @agw5425
text: I´m afraid you got your calculations wrong, a 2,5 kW heater will always be 2,5 kW if you drop the voltage it will try to draw not 10 A not 0,5A but 200A to achieve it´s designed rating. Only if you actively limit the current will it change output. That is why the cable to a car starter is as thick as your finger to handle the load, a motor of the same power but at 240 V would need a far thinner wire for the same Wattage effect. It is far easier to cause a fire with 12 v and to thin cables than with 240V. Just so you know, you got ohm´s law wrong, that can be verry dangerous if viewers try this. The losses with a peltier is huge, you get far more out with a regular led acid battery and near 0 chance to burn yourself. The closest to useful output is if you have at least a oil barrels volume with sand and 30+ cm of insulation on all sides hooked up to waste heat/power. There is a reason "polar night energy" uses containers the volume of grain silos for heat storage.

author: @toddharshbarger8616
text:
Great demo!  I’m interested a bit more about the limits and losses.  Sand is cheap and has an enormous capacity but isn’t there a point at which depending on the gradient energy can no longer be deposited?  If for example rather than the electrical heat element you ran a water based heat exchanger - in that case since water has the 100degree phase change barrier you’d limit the system well beneath the sands capacity.  Similarly if the extraction process has such limits it may impose limits.  Finally I am curious about heat loss phenomena.  The main advantage to phase change systems is that you don’t get the heat gradient penalty - with any imperfect insulation the heat energy lost is related to the heat gradient meaning if sand is heated to its higher capacity won’t this exacerbate gradient heat losses in all but perfect insulators?

I hope you might explore these aspects as you are certain to add far better clarity then my own convoluted understanding!

Thanks again.

author: @Loyd-d8d
text:
Sand is not an optimal material for storing heat. More available are shale, rock and clay, which also are more dense and have higher specific heat.

Example, clay specific heat 1.38kJ/kg'C, density 1.6kg/L (source: engineeringtoolbox). When building home, one could dig foundation slightly deeper, insulate sides and bottom, fill original dirt back, add circulation loop, insulate top, build floor. For 10x20m house and 1m deep, it would store 440MJ/'C=122kWh/'C. Concrete can go as high as 70'C, so for this 10x20m house range 20-70'C provides 6MWh of heat capacity, may be enough to heat this home through winter.

text: Hello excuse in advance my English I am a French. I have heard of the sand battery but the central question for me is this: what thermal insulation uses its batteries to be able as it is announced by the company that created them to keep the heat for months? it seems to me that we are orders of magnitude away from what is possible with glass wool or rock wool and the achievable thicknesses knowing that the thickness of the thermal insulation layer has an importance which decreases with its reverse. That is to say that the greater the thickness, the further the external layer is, but the more it is, the more the exchange surface of this layer with the outside increases. So increasing the thickness of the insulation layer has less and less effect. If R is the thermal resistance, r1 the internal diameter of the insulation layer and r2 its external diameter, we have R proportional to (1/r1 - 1/r2). When r2 tends to infinity only tends to count the inside diameter r1. Raising R2 no longer serves any purpose.

author: @charanvantijn541
text:
Hi RMS, What did you do to us? Taking a 12 volt 6 watt heating element and heating sand for two hours? You loose way more than 6 watts in radiation alone. So the sand would get luke warm at best. 
Another way of looking at it... You have about a liter of sand. A liter of sand weighs about 4 kg. To get that to 200 C, it needs about 800 * 4 * 200 = 640000 Joule = 0.177 kWh. This means you need 88 watts for two hours to get the sand to heat to 200 C without any heat loss. Way more than your 6 watt element with heat loss.

author: @maxtorque2277
text: BTW, the thermal battery boosting the COP of a heat pump only works in climates where the solar load is anti-phase to the heating demand. That thermal store lets you capture (some) heat from the sun during the day,a nd use it in the evening / at night to boost a heat pump when you actually want the heating ie when the sun has gone down.  If you use a heat pump for cooling (ie a Air conditioner, then clearly you don't need the storage because the sun is what is causing the thermal load ......  ;-)

author: @maxtorque2277
text:
er, if you do the maths, then you'll find that this battery is so in-efficient, and so expensive per output watt, that it's effectively negative in terms of return!  Your 6 watt heater (144Wh per day if driven 24 hours, ie worth about 2 pence)  is not just pathetically low powered, but also has far to small a surface area to heat up a material with such a low thermal co-efficient ( it normally heats water, which is a) a liquid and hence has convection, but also b) boils and uses the latent heat of vapourisation to pull that large (2kW) of power from it. Stick it in sand, and you'll almost certainly find the heating element life is very short because the element will run above its oxidisation temperature.  Using peltier elements to get the heat out as electricity is also massively flawed on any practical DIY scale. The elements are very expensive per watt, and have low efficiency, so you now have to cool the "cold side" and in doing so, loose something like 75% of the tiny amount of energy you stored in the sand in the first place. 

Unfortunately, like wind turbines, sand batteries simply don't scale very well. And of course, there is nothing new, as we used storage heaters for years,which are just electrical elements surrounded by silica bricks, ie sand..... ;-)

BTW, there is one way that you might be able to get a "sand battery" or similar low temperature thermal store to work on a small scale, and this is to act as a heta pump booster to reduced the deltaT of the pump, and hence boost the COP. Whilst the energy in the sand battery isn't sufficient to actually heat your house, it is sufficent when done on a larger scale (eg a couple of tonnes of sand) to boost COP and hence improve a heat pumps transfer efficiency

author: @MikesAllotment
text: Another great video Rob 👍.  I'm loving the idea of sand batteries and think your use of a kettle element is 'awesome!'.  As you point out - getting the energy into the sand battery is relatively easy, but only half the story.  Getting it out again in the form of 'useful' electrical energy is more difficult.  Realistically, how many volts and how much current can you get out of those Peltier modules?  If you do manage to get a sand battery up to x-hundred degrees - how realistic is it to get that energy back out as enough electrical energy that you can do something practical with - e.g. charge a mobile phone / tablet for example?   Spinning a tiddly little propeller motor is fine for a demo, but can you get a 'decent' amount of voltage/current from a Peltier (or multiple Peltiers in parallel and/or series) for some real-world practical use?  If not, are there any other practical ways to recover that stored energy as a useful amount of electrical energy?   e.g. powering led lights or a security camera in a shed down the allotment which would require ~400mA ?  Genuinely interested in this as I think they have massive potential... especially when you can 'charge' the battery using both solar and wind power...

   But yeah, sand is definitely easier, safer, and more convenient.

author: @bobkelly2447
text:
I find it odd that all this talk about sand batteries when using Water as a thermal storage medium is so much better !
When I lived in Reno, Nv. there was a company that made houses that had big windows facing south and large tanks of water just inside the glass in the house.... the entire south facing wall was glass and behind that glass was these barrels of water from cealing to floor. the efficiency of these houses was nothing short of remarkable ! they used very little energy to heat the home in the winter and during the hot summer a opaque tarp was raised to shade the tanks from the sun light and then opened at night so they cooled the house in the day time
it was so neet I almost bought one of those houses !  most were all automated but way beyond my budget at the time.  the developer bragged that less than $20 dollars was needed to heat the place in the winter and no money for cooling in the summer.  I would say that that was as close to a perfect house as you could ever get....

So I’ve been thinking about this. I have a degree in glass art and taught it for a few years. The sand battery tech is similar to electric glass blowing equipment. So My idea is to get an electric glass kiln used for glassblowing and add a crucible filled with sand in it and hook the elements up to solar panels.

author: @jamesross1003
text: We did something similar in a college physics class.  The class built it with salts rather than sand.  We heated the salt until it melted then placed it in an insulated container.   I believe it was epsom salts but can't remember for sure.  It stayed hot for days.  We slow cooked a ham using the residual heat the next day.  We had a lot of fun in that class.  Do your research on safety folks.  Notice Rob is using kiln dried sand.  If it is not properly dry moisture will come off of what you are heating, therefore do not enclose it in an airtight container to heat it.  It might build up pressure otherwise and might rupture or on cooling collapse your vessel.  Rob's playing with fire video applies to all things, not just fire.  I really enjoy these kinds of videos.  Thanks Rob!

replies.author: @inso80
replies.text: With salts you get the additional energy storage in the phase transition of the molten/solid salt. but on the negative side, if that molten salt gets in contact with water, you get a pretty nasty surprise. I have played with the thought that could run a furnace exhaust through a sand battery for additional gains, if you use wood, gas or other combustion heaters.

author: @mikehart2555
text: Lovely video! It seems that you get to play with the kind of stuff that the rest of us would like to. A couple of points though... Firstly, with water the element is limited in the temperature it can get up to. It will be a little over 100C, "over" because there is a gaseous envelope (steam) around the element. However, sand can reach far higher temperatures, as you have said. With a constant electrical feed this temperature will be defined by losses but also by the thermal conductivity of the sand i.e. does the sand insulate the element or does it leach the heat away, like a heatsink. A comparison/experiment to show this thermal gradient would be an interesting development. Secondly, I would caution about feeding this sand battery with electricity directly from solar panels. The heating element wont care about DC or AC, but any thermostat that you use will have to have contacts rated for the correct Direct Current, which will be far more of a challenge than a simple AC rating. The devices you have shown, for example, (thermal fuse and thermostat) are unlikely to handle a practical installation with a 10 panel array and couple/three cubic meters of sand. Imagine that poor thermal fuse breaking 300v at 5 amps or more. The arc would be impressive as the device died! Love the video, and would love you to take it further.

author: @objection_your_honor
text:
Water is the only natural molecule that take the most amount of energy to rise 1 degree in temperature.
When it does get that energy, it releases it the slowest of all molecules. 
This is why it's always warmer by the lake in winter and cooler in summer.
That huge body of water is acting like a battery and releasing its heat slowly, while all the rocks around have lost their heat by the middle of the night. 
That's why deserts are so cold at night. They piss away all their heat quickly. Not so with water.

author: @splashcam
text: Your channel is fantastic! I built a 4-ft x 8-ft solar air heater that gets up to about 250F on a clear sunny day here in Texas.  I also built a 4-ft insulated sand battery cube that has an "inner air coil" of 4-inch aluminum flex duct , and an "outer air coil" with about a ton of sand all around and in-between the air coils.  Using a solar panel and a battery and a 12v marine bilge fan, I've been blowing hot air into the inner coil in tests and have been able to heat the inner core of the sand battery to over 100F using only solar heated air and solar-charged battery power.  Can use the outer coil with another fan to take heat out of the sand battery into my shop!  Next, I'll be adding a 12v resistive heating element to the system to see if I can get the internal temp to about 200F.  Thanks for all the ideas!

Depends on the dirt, of course.. if you use something that is clay rich, you can get it wet and pack it well. Adding sand to the wet 'dirt' will make it into a cob like material that can be packed or formed. This will make for a quite good mass, better than just sand.

author: @michaeldibb
text:
The specific heat capacity of water is 4.18kJ/kg/C, whereas sand is is about 0.8kJ/kg/C so for each 1kg of water it can store about 5.2 times as much energy than sand.  Another way of looking at it is 1kg of water at 100C with have the same energy content as 1kg sand at 520C  (or 5.2kg of sand at 100C)!
There's a good Youtube video that compare water ice cubes with stainless steel ice cubes,  'Are Steel Ice Cubes Better Than Regular Ice?' .  It's really surprising how much more energy dense water has of steel.

author: @charanvantijn541
text: So you have read the BBC article too. Could you please add some more info about getting the heat out. You can't simply stick a copper pipe with water in there when it is above the boiling point of water. You would get steam out of it. The other thing is insulation. Polystyrene would melt. Glass fibre would be good for the top and sides, but not for the bottom because the sand would flatten it.

replies.author: @NdxtremePro
replies.text:
You CAN just put copper in with some water. You aren't factoring in the rate of flow for the water. The faster the water flows through the pipe, the less heat it is able to absorb. So you calculate the rate of flow you need in order to keep the water at the desired temp. You can also mix the water with cooler water after it gets out of the battery, so that it returns to liquid.
There are also uses for the steam, such as turning a traditional generator, if that is your use case.

text:
That might be an idea to incorporate into your rocket heater.
The main problem (imo) is that you are chucking a lot of heat in quickly whereas you usually want the heat to be dissipated slower.
Storage radiators get round the problem by heating the core to a read heat and having it insulated so it feels nice and warm. To make it a bit better they have air vents running through the core with a cap balanced with a bi-metallic plate allowing you to set a desired temp and have the heat dumped quickly until that temp is reached and then shut down to trickle!
Of course sand would need a tube to allow air through it .... but that should not be a problem for a manufacturer (or a man with a welder) ;o)

6 W of heater per £5 kettle.
Cheap, optimal, big sand brings opportunity and slow with buildup, what countryside greenhouse or large shed could benefit?
Could a biopool be heated 3 times per year?
Could I build a hobbit house and heat it in 7 minutes with a simple ring of pipe's around the house. Cold to home in a short time is a sort of magic if you've ever been cold.

The next question is, what is the cheapest way to supply each of those units with power.

author: @richardamullens
text:
Specific heat of quartz 0.7 joules/gram/degree C.  Specific heat of water 4.18 joules/gram/degree C.

So, weight for weight, water can store 6 times as much heat. Therefore you would need to heat the sand from 20 to  500C to store as much energy as heating water from 20 to 100C.

Density of quartz 2.65 g/cc - density of dry sand 1.6.

A possible advantage of sand is that the air between the sand particles would lower the heat loss.

I use a block of Serpentinite (density 2.6), heated in a microwave, then surrounded with insulation in place of a hot water bottle. Like sand it has the advantage that it doesn't evaporate.

Then, the Bismuth in the Peltier Effect Device is going to melt at high temperatures.

But a higher temperature would give a greater thermodynamic efficiency.

author: @jamaraquai4224
text: After we saw the giant Finnish model, we built a sand battery for our bus-turned-tiny-house since propane heating gets expensive quickly, wood burning presents issues with ventilation, and we always have more solar power than we have use for. Currently I have the battery set up to be plugged in and turned on/off with a cheapo Christmas tree foot switch, and it's been wonderful and so much more efficient than I could've possibly imagined. A single, tiny space heater will drain our +300ah battery bank in a matter of hours, and all that heat disappears the second it's turned off. With the sand battery, though, we can leave it on all night, get the bus nice and toasty, and for upwards of 5 hours after it's been shut off we can *keep* the bus warm and cozy, and all without making so much as a dent on our battery bank. On top of that, should we get a week of rubbish weather where we're not getting a full recharge on the LiFe batteries, our baby alcohol burners under the battery heat it up just as well and, again, at a fraction of the price of propane. I had the same idea to use a series of peltier devices to reclaim some of the heat energy and put in into a heat dump for our hot water. Still need to sort out the logistics of that project, but we've been wonderfully pleased with our sand-in-steel heater. ^.^

replies.author: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN
replies.text:
Ethanol has an energy value of 29.7 MJ/KG compared to propane and butane at around 50MJ/KG. Gas if purchased in bottles over 6kg (the bigger the cheaper) for me, the price of Ethanol, even buying in bulk...gas works out much better.
If you don't mind me asking, but where and for how much cost do you get your alcohol jamaraquai4224? I cannot get a Good deal on it anywhere.
Even purchasing Small cannisters of butane, adding up the weight/calorific value, the small cannisters of butane work out better value for me; purchased in bulk of-course.

replies.author: @jamaraquai4224
replies.text: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN I always have a supply of denatured alcohol on hand for woodworking. Locally it's roughly $15/gal, and after multiple tests, the alcohol burners burn through roughly 1/2c of alcohol in 2hrs. The sand battery heats up with the burners in 30mins. Make no mistake: I'm not saying that heating the sand battery with alcohol burners is more efficient than using butane, propane, or certainly gas, but ethanol burns clean where gas does not, and leading a mobile and minimal lifestyle, I don't have the luxury of hauling around multiple tanks of fuel. We use excess solar power to charge our sand battery: the burners work extremely well in a pinch, tho. (:

replies.author: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN
replies.text:
@jamaraquai4224 I guess it was the "at a fraction of the price of propane" made me wonder.
Yes, Ethanol burns clean and I do have a couple of gallons of the stuff for my alcohol stoves.
Butane is a rather safe gas compared to propane (less pressure) and should burn clean like alcohol giving off carbon dioxide and H2O (good old water).
Like any flame, complete combustion is what we're after jamaraquai.
I guess if it's only now and then, using the alcohol stove to heat the sand battery, it's not too bad.
Here in Northern Ireland, we'd never get enough solar for use in a sand battery for heating when a Good heater is really needed unfortunately.
Anyway, all the best jamaraquai 👍

replies.author: @jamaraquai4224
replies.text: As a fellow off-gridder who has already built a sand battery, just bear in mind, that the larger your battery, the more time and energy it will take to "power" it. Obviously we have no idea what kind of excess energy you're working with, but a 55gal drum will take a pretty significant amount of time and energy to heat up sufficiently to warm a house and/or heat water. If you're constantly dumping energy into it, then you'll be fine (eventually), but if you can only feed the battery intermittently as is often the case with solar/wind power, then don't forget to plan way ahead for your shower. ;)

author: @justtinkering6713
text:
The problem I see with sand batteries and flywheels is even if you don't use the stored energy it eventually runs down and the energy is lost. Also the energy you extract in the beginning is more than you get it the end, it runs down.
But with gravity storage of energy, the energy can be stored forever until you're ready to use it, and it gives the same power at the end as it did in the beginning.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: for sure

replies.author: @BB-sm8ey
replies.text: There are less expensive, more energy dense ways to achieve near lossless storage. Check out phase change materials and sorption for instance.

replies.author: @jamaraquai4224
replies.text: That's why people typically use sand batteries as dumps for excess energy instead of a primary source of energy. Fortunately, as a sand battery scales up in size, both the amount of energy it's able to store and the length of time it's able to store that energy increase exponentially (to a point), but that's not exactly practical for the average Joe.
